issues.claim: The genotype-to-phenotype ratio is one-two-one for both
issues.problem: Awkward phrasing — 'genotype-to-phenotype ratio' is non-standard terminology. The intended meaning is that both incomplete dominance and codominance yield a 1:2:1 phenotypic ratio (because the heterozygote is phenotypically distinguishable) AND a 1:2:1 genotypic ratio. Factually defensible but could confuse a careful AP student parsing 'genotype-to-phenotype' as a mapping rather than 'both the genotype and phenotype ratios are 1:2:1.'
issues.suggested_fix: Rephrase: 'Both the genotype and phenotype ratios are one-two-one — but the heterozygote looks completely different in each case.'

issues.claim: independent assortment in Metaphase One ... giving two to the twenty-third — about eight point four million — combinations per human gamete
issues.problem: The 2^23 figure is the number of possible chromosome combinations arising from independent assortment alone (ignoring crossing over). The phrasing 'combinations per gamete' is slightly ambiguous — any single gamete contains ONE combination; 2^23 is the size of the possible outcome space. AP graders generally accept this colloquial framing, but a strict reading could be marked imprecise.
issues.suggested_fix: Reword to: 'giving two to the twenty-third — about eight point four million — possible chromosome combinations across gametes.'

overall_assessment: Module is factually sound and AP-aligned. Mendel's three laws, monohybrid/dihybrid ratios, incomplete dominance vs. codominance, ABO genetics, pleiotropy/polygenic/sex-linked patterns, meiotic mechanics (reductional vs. equational), and aneuploidy examples (Trisomy 21, XXY Klinefelter, XO Turner) are all accurate. Only two minor wording nits flagged; no hallucinations or rigor failures. Safe to publish.
